title: GoogleMapsAPI.jl
text:
What’s covered
A Julia wrapper for the Google Maps Platform web APIs with feature parity to the official googlemaps Python client — plus typed wrapper outputs that play nicely with GeoInterface.jl and a Makie plotting extension.

title: GoogleMapsAPI.jl
text:
Routes API — compute_routes, compute_route_matrix
Geocoding API — geocode, reverse_geocode
Elevation API — elevation, elevation_along_path
Geolocation API — geolocate
Time Zone API — timezone
Roads API — snap_to_roads, nearest_roads, speed_limits, snapped_speed_limits
Places API (legacy) — find_place, places, places_nearby, place, places_photo, places_autocomplete, places_autocomplete_query
Maps Static API — static_map
Address Validation API — addressvalidation
Polyline utilities — encode_polyline, decode_polyline
Typed wrappers — GMPoint, GMLineString, GMFeature, GMFeatureCollection with GeoInterface trait conformance
mapplot — Static-Maps background + scatter/line overlay for any response (via the Makie extension)

title: GoogleMapsAPI.jl
text:
Client infrastructure
Mirrors the Python client: retry with jittered backoff on 5xx / OVER_QUERY_LIMIT, QPS/QPM rate limiting, HMAC-SHA1 URL signing for enterprise client_id / client_secret credentials, and the X-Goog-Maps-Experience-ID header.

title: GoogleMapsAPI.jl
text:
Next steps

Getting started — install, authenticate, make a first call.
Plotting — mapplot, plus GeoInterface/GeoMakie integration.
Types — GMPoint, GMLineString, GMFeature, GMFeatureCollection, and the extractor functions that normalise any response.
API — every exported name with its docstring.

title: Getting started
text: Install

title: Getting started
text:
using Pkg
Pkg.add(url = "https://github.com/joshday/GoogleMapsAPI.jl")

title: Getting started
text:
Authenticate
Every function reads ENV["GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY"] by default. Export it in your shell:
export GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY="your-key-here"
Or pass key = "..." (or a preconfigured client = GoogleMapsClient(...)) to any call.

title: Getting started
text:
First call
using GoogleMapsAPI

r = geocode("1600 Amphitheatre Pkwy, Mountain View, CA")
r.results[1].geometry.location   # (lat = ..., lng = ...)

route = compute_routes(
    (35.9132, -79.0822),            # Carrboro, NC
    (38.9805, -77.0915);            # Bethesda, MD
    routing_preference = "TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL",
    departure_time     = "2026-04-27T08:00:00-04:00",
)
parse_duration(route.routes[1].duration)   # seconds
route.routes[1].distanceMeters             # meters

title: Getting started
text:
Reuse a client
For longer-running scripts, construct a GoogleMapsClient once so that retry / rate-limit state is shared across calls:
c = GoogleMapsClient(
    key                    = ENV["GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY"],
    timeout                = 30,
    retry_timeout          = 60,
    queries_per_second     = 60,
    queries_per_minute     = 6000,
    retry_over_query_limit = true,
    experience_id          = "my-analytics-id",
)

geocode("1600 Amphitheatre Pkwy, Mountain View, CA"; client = c)
elevation((27.9881, 86.9250); client = c)
Per-call overrides (key=, timeout=) do not mutate the shared client; they share the rate-limit state.
Enterprise signing uses client_id + client_secret:
c = GoogleMapsClient(
    client_id     = "gme-myorg",
    client_secret = "vNIXE0xscrmjlyV-12Nj_BvUPaw=",
)

title: Getting started
text:
Exceptions

ApiError(status, message) — non-OK status in the response envelope (e.g. "OVER_QUERY_LIMIT", "REQUEST_DENIED").
HTTPError(status) — unexpected HTTP status on endpoints without an envelope.
TransportError(cause) — network / DNS / unexpected exception.
RequestTimeout() — total elapsed time exceeded client.retry_timeout.

title: Getting started
text:
Where to next

Plotting — mapplot, and using any response directly with GeoInterface or GeoMakie.
Types — features(response) turns any response into a normalised iterable of GMFeatures.

title: Plotting
text:
The built-in mapplot
GoogleMapsAPI.jl ships two plotting paths:
Load Makie (plus a backend and FileIO) alongside GoogleMapsAPI, and mapplot(x) fetches a static map sized to the response’s bounding box and overlays everything it can extract.

title: Plotting
text:
A geocoded address

r = geocode("1600 Amphitheatre Pkwy, Mountain View, CA")
mapplot(r)






A computed route, on satellite imagery

route = compute_routes(
    (35.9132, -79.0822),            # Carrboro, NC
    (38.9805, -77.0915);            # Bethesda, MD
    routing_preference = "TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL",
)
mapplot(route; maptype = "satellite", size = (800, 600))






Elevation samples along a transect

elevations = elevation([(38.5, -120.2), (40.7, -120.95), (43.25, -126.45)])
mapplot(elevations; maptype = "terrain")






Styling kwargs








Kwarg
Default
Purpose




maptype
"roadmap"
"roadmap", "satellite", "terrain", "hybrid"


size
(640, 640)
Image pixel dimensions


padding
0.15
Fraction of bbox span added as margin


markercolor
:red
Scatter colour for points


markersize
12
Scatter size


linecolor
:dodgerblue
Polyline colour


linewidth
3
Polyline width



Everything else — scale, language, region, style, client, key, timeout, etc. — is forwarded to static_map.


How it works
Internally, mapplot:

Calls features(x) → extracts every GMPoint and GMLineString.
Computes a bounding box and a Web-Mercator-fit zoom level.
Fetches the Static Maps image via static_map(...) and decodes the PNG bytes with FileIO.
Draws the image, then scatters markers and draws polylines at their projected pixel coordinates — so overlays line up with the real map.

Because it goes through features(x), any response type works — even a raw JSON.Object you built yourself.

title: Plotting
text:
GeoInterface: use responses anywhere
The real integration happens one level up. The four wrapper types (GMPoint, GMLineString, GMFeature, GMFeatureCollection) implement the GeoInterface traits, so every GeoInterface-aware package sees them as native geometries.

Traits

using GeoInterface

p = GMPoint(37.4, -122.1)
GeoInterface.isgeometry(p), GeoInterface.geomtrait(p),
GeoInterface.x(p), GeoInterface.y(p)

(true, PointTrait(), -122.1, 37.4)



ls = GMLineString([GMPoint(0.0, 0.0), GMPoint(1.0, 2.0), GMPoint(3.0, 4.0)])
GeoInterface.geomtrait(ls), GeoInterface.ngeom(ls), GeoInterface.getgeom(ls, 2)

(LineStringTrait(), 3, GMPoint(lat=1.0, lng=2.0))



fc = features(geocode("Carrboro, NC"))
f  = GeoInterface.getfeature(fc, 1)
(GeoInterface.geometry(f), GeoInterface.properties(f).formatted_address)

(GMPoint(lat=35.9101438, lng=-79.0752895), "Carrboro, NC, USA")




What this unlocks
Anything in the JuliaGeo ecosystem that accepts GeoInterface geometries now accepts GoogleMapsAPI responses — including:

GeoMakie.jl — geographic plotting with map projections (see the next section).
GeoJSON.jl — serialise a feature collection to GeoJSON.
ArchGDAL.jl — reproject, transform, or export to shapefile/GeoPackage/etc.
GeometryOps.jl — distance, area, simplification, clipping.
Shapefile.jl, Tables.jl-backed workflows, etc.

title: Plotting
text:
GeoMakie integration
GeoMakie plots lat/lng data on a GeoAxis that handles projections. Because GMPoint implements PointTrait, you can pull points out of any response and hand them straight to Makie.

using GeoMakie


Scatter: extract points, plot on a projected axis

r = places("coffee"; location = (37.7749, -122.4194), radius = 2000)
pts = points(r)

fig = Figure(size = (800, 500))
ax  = GeoAxis(fig[1, 1]; dest = "+proj=natearth",
              xticks = -180:30:180, yticks = -90:30:90)
lines!(ax, GeoMakie.coastlines(); color = :grey70, linewidth = 0.5)
scatter!(ax,
    [GeoInterface.x(p) for p in pts],
    [GeoInterface.y(p) for p in pts];
    color = :crimson, markersize = 10, strokecolor = :white, strokewidth = 1,
)
xlims!(ax, -123, -122)
ylims!(ax, 37.5, 38.0)
fig






Routes: lines on a projected axis

route = compute_routes((35.9132, -79.0822), (38.9805, -77.0915))

fig = Figure(size = (800, 500))
ax  = GeoAxis(fig[1, 1]; dest = "+proj=natearth",
              xticks = -180:5:180, yticks = -90:5:90)
lines!(ax, GeoMakie.coastlines(); color = :grey70, linewidth = 0.5)
for ls in linestrings(route)
    lines!(ax,
        [GeoInterface.x(pt) for pt in ls],
        [GeoInterface.y(pt) for pt in ls];
        color = :dodgerblue, linewidth = 3,
    )
end
xlims!(ax, -81, -76)
ylims!(ax, 35, 40)
fig






One function for anything
Treat a response generically — plot every point or every line without branching on which endpoint produced it — by walking the feature collection:

function geoplot!(ax, x; kwargs...)
    for f in features(x)
        g = GeoInterface.geometry(f)
        if g isa GMPoint
            scatter!(ax, [GeoInterface.x(g)], [GeoInterface.y(g)]; kwargs...)
        elseif g isa GMLineString
            lines!(ax,
                [GeoInterface.x(pt) for pt in g],
                [GeoInterface.y(pt) for pt in g];
                kwargs...,
            )
        end
    end
    ax
end

fig = Figure(size = (800, 500))
ax  = GeoAxis(fig[1, 1]; dest = "+proj=natearth",
              xticks = -180:15:180, yticks = -90:15:90)
lines!(ax, GeoMakie.coastlines(); color = :grey70, linewidth = 0.5)
geoplot!(ax, geocode("San Francisco, CA"); color = :crimson, markersize = 14)
geoplot!(ax, compute_routes((37.77, -122.42), (40.71, -74.01));
             color = :dodgerblue, linewidth = 2)
geoplot!(ax, elevation([(40, -120), (42, -122), (44, -124)]);
             color = :orange, markersize = 10)
xlims!(ax, -130, -65)
ylims!(ax, 25, 50)
fig






When to use what







Goal
Use




Quick look at a response on a real base map
mapplot(x)


Publication-quality projection / custom styling
GeoMakie + wrappers


Hand data to another geospatial tool (GeoJSON, GDAL, …)
features(x) or points(x) / linestrings(x)



mapplot is great for exploration; GeoMakie + GeoInterface is the right path when you want to compose with other layers, apply a projection, or integrate a response into a larger visualisation.

title: Types & extractors
text:
The wrapper types
Every GoogleMapsAPI response is a raw JSON object (JSON.Object) — great for exploratory access via dot notation, but awkward to hand to the wider geospatial ecosystem.
GoogleMapsAPI.jl ships four lightweight wrapper types plus a set of extractor functions that normalise any response shape into the same structure.
using GoogleMapsAPI

title: Types & extractors
text:
GMPoint
A single lat/lng pair.

p = GMPoint(37.4, -122.1)

GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1)




GMLineString
An ordered sequence of GMPoints — produced whenever a response carries an encoded polyline (e.g. a route from compute_routes).

ls = GMLineString([GMPoint(0.0, 0.0), GMPoint(1.0, 2.0), GMPoint(3.0, 4.0)])
length(ls), collect(ls)

(3, GMPoint[GMPoint(lat=0.0, lng=0.0), GMPoint(lat=1.0, lng=2.0), GMPoint(lat=3.0, lng=4.0)])




GMFeature
Geometry (a GMPoint, GMLineString, or nothing) paired with the raw response object that yielded it — so you keep access to fields like formatted_address, placeId, name, elevation, duration, etc.

f = GMFeature(p, (; formatted_address = "Mountain View, CA"))
f.geometry, f.properties

(GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1), (formatted_address = "Mountain View, CA",))




GMFeatureCollection
Iterable container of GMFeatures with length, indexing, and iteration.

fc = GMFeatureCollection([f, GMFeature(nothing, (; note = "no geometry"))])
length(fc), fc[1].geometry

(2, GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1))

title: Types & extractors
text:
features(response) — normalise any response
features walks any GoogleMapsAPI return value and produces a GMFeatureCollection. It handles:

geocode / reverse_geocode — results[i].geometry.location → points.
compute_routes — routes[i].polyline.encodedPolyline → linestrings.
places, places_nearby, find_place — results/candidates[i].geometry.location.
place — single result.geometry.location.
places_autocomplete — predictions (no geometry, just properties).
elevation, snap_to_roads, nearest_roads — bare arrays of {location, …} objects.
geolocate — single {location, accuracy}.
Any object with an unrecognised shape is wrapped as a single-feature collection with nothing geometry and the raw object as properties.


using JSON

# Synthetic geocode-shaped response for illustration
r = JSON.parse("""
{
    "status":"OK",
    "results":[
        {"geometry":{"location":{"lat":37.4,"lng":-122.1}},"formatted_address":"A"},
        {"geometry":{"location":{"lat":40.0,"lng":-74.0}},"formatted_address":"B"}
    ]
}
""")

fc = features(r)

GMFeatureCollection(2 features)



fc[1].geometry

GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1)



fc[1].properties.formatted_address

"A"

title: Types & extractors
text:
Convenience extractors
For the common cases you don’t need the full feature wrapper — just the points or the polylines.

points(r)

2-element Vector{GMPoint}:
 GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1)
 GMPoint(lat=40.0, lng=-74.0)



route = JSON.parse("""{"routes":[{"polyline":{"encodedPolyline":"_p~iF~ps|U_ulLnnqC_mqNvxq`@"}}]}""")
linestrings(route)

1-element Vector{GMLineString}:
 GMLineString(3 points)



geometries(r)

2-element Vector{GMPoint}:
 GMPoint(lat=37.4, lng=-122.1)
 GMPoint(lat=40.0, lng=-74.0)

title: Types & extractors
text:
Why wrappers?
Three reasons:

Type stability. GMPoint is a concrete struct — accessing p.lat costs nothing. Walking into a JSON.Object hits dynamic dispatch.
GeoInterface. These wrapper types implement GeoInterface.jl traits, so any GeoInterface-aware package (GeoMakie, GeoJSON, ArchGDAL, Shapefile, GeometryOps, …) can consume GoogleMapsAPI responses directly. See Plotting.
Normalisation. features(x) lets downstream code iterate uniformly without branching on which endpoint produced x.

title: API
text: Types

title: API
text:
ApiError


ApiError(status, message=nothing)
A Google Maps API call returned a non-success status in the response body (e.g. "OVER_QUERY_LIMIT", "INVALID_REQUEST").




GMFeature


GMFeature(geometry, properties)
Geometry (a GMPoint, GMLineString, or nothing) paired with the raw response object that yielded it, so callers can still reach into fields like name, place_id, formatted_address, elevation, etc.




GMFeatureCollection


GMFeatureCollection(features)
Iterable collection of GMFeatures. Produced by features.




GMLineString


GMLineString(points)
An ordered sequence of GMPoints — e.g. the decoded polyline of a route.




GMPoint


GMPoint(lat, lng)
A single lat/lng point extracted from a GoogleMapsAPI response.




GoogleMapsClient


GoogleMapsClient(; key, client_id, client_secret, ...) -> GoogleMapsClient
Container for Google Maps Platform credentials and per-connection options. Primary use: pass to any flat-function call via client = ... instead of repeating key=, timeout=, etc.

Keyword Arguments

key: Maps API key (required unless client_id + client_secret set).
client_id, client_secret: enterprise Maps-for-Work credentials. client_secret must be the base64-URL-safe-encoded signing secret.
channel: enterprise tracking channel. Alphanumeric ._- only.
timeout: read timeout per HTTP call (seconds, default 30).
retry_timeout: total time budget across retries (seconds, default 60).
queries_per_second, queries_per_minute: rate-limit caps; client sleeps to stay under the tighter of the two.
retry_over_query_limit: whether to retry on OVER_QUERY_LIMIT / Roads RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED. Default true.
experience_id: value for X-Goog-Maps-Experience-ID header.
base_url: default API host (https://maps.googleapis.com).
transport, sleep: runtime hooks, injectable for tests.






HTTPError


HTTPError(status)
An unexpected HTTP status was returned (non-200 on an endpoint that doesn’t wrap its errors in the API-status envelope).




RequestTimeout


RequestTimeout()
Total time across attempts exceeded client.retry_timeout.




TransportError


TransportError(cause)
Something went wrong while executing the HTTP request (DNS, connection reset, etc.). cause holds the underlying exception.

title: API
text:
Functions


addressvalidation


addressvalidation(address_lines; region_code=nothing, locality=nothing,
                  enable_usps_cass=false, ...) -> JSON.Object
Validate a postal address. address_lines is a string (wrapped to a one-element vector) or a vector of address-line strings. enable_usps_cass is US/PR only.




api_key


api_key() -> String
Return the Google Maps API key from ENV["GOOGLE_MAPS_API_KEY"]. Throws if unset.

Examples
key = GoogleMapsAPI.api_key()





clear_experience_id! {#clear_experience_id!}


clear_experience_id!(client) — clear the experience id.




compute_route_matrix


compute_route_matrix(origins, destinations; kwargs...) -> JSON.Array
Call the Google Routes API v2:computeRouteMatrix endpoint.
origins and destinations are vectors of waypoints (accepted forms as for waypoint). Returns a flat array of matrix elements keyed by originIndex / destinationIndex.

Keyword Arguments
See compute_routes; supports travel_mode, routing_preference, departure_time, arrival_time, traffic_model, language_code, region_code, units, field_mask, extra, client, key, timeout.





compute_routes


compute_routes(origin, destination; kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Call the Google Routes API v2:computeRoutes endpoint.
origin and destination are converted with waypoint and may be (lat, lon) tuples, address strings, or waypoint Dicts (for placeId, etc.).

Keyword Arguments

travel_mode: "DRIVE" (default), "BICYCLE", "WALK", "TWO_WHEELER", "TRANSIT".
routing_preference: "TRAFFIC_AWARE" (default), "TRAFFIC_AWARE_OPTIMAL", "TRAFFIC_UNAWARE".
departure_time: ISO 8601 timestamp (e.g. "2026-04-27T08:00:00-04:00").
arrival_time: ISO 8601 timestamp (transit only).
traffic_model: "BEST_GUESS", "OPTIMISTIC", or "PESSIMISTIC".
intermediates: vector of waypoints visited between origin and destination.
compute_alternative_routes::Bool = false.
units: "METRIC" or "IMPERIAL".
language_code, region_code: IETF BCP-47 / CLDR codes.
field_mask: comma-separated response field mask (default covers duration, staticDuration, distanceMeters, and encodedPolyline).
extra::AbstractDict: merged into the request body as a last step — use for advanced options such as "routeModifiers", "extraComputations", etc.
client::GoogleMapsClient: optional; see GoogleMapsClient.
key: API key (default api_key()).
timeout: read timeout in seconds (default 30).






decode_polyline


decode_polyline(s) -> Vector{NamedTuple{(:lat, :lng), Tuple{Float64, Float64}}}
Decode a Google-Maps polyline string into a vector of (lat, lng) named tuples.




elevation


elevation(locations; kwargs...) -> JSON.Array
Provides elevation data for locations, a single location or a list of lat/lng values (tuples, named tuples, dicts).

Keyword Arguments

client::GoogleMapsClient: optional.
key, timeout: shortcut kwargs as for compute_routes.






elevation_along_path


elevation_along_path(path, samples; kwargs...) -> JSON.Array
Provides elevation data sampled along path. path may be an encoded polyline string (prepended with "enc:" internally) or a list of lat/lng values. samples is the number of sample points.




encode_polyline


encode_polyline(points) -> String
Encode a list of lat/lng points into a Google-Maps polyline string. Each point may be a (lat, lng) tuple, a NamedTuple, or an AbstractDict.
See https://developers.google.com/maps/documentation/utilities/polylinealgorithm




features


features(x) -> GMFeatureCollection
Normalise any GoogleMapsAPI response into a GMFeatureCollection. Handles the usual results / routes / candidates / predictions / snappedPoints wrappers, plus single-feature responses (geolocate, place → result) and raw arrays (elevation, snap_to_roads).




find_place


find_place(input, input_type; fields=nothing, location_bias=nothing, language=nothing, kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Find a place by text input. input_type must be "textquery" or "phonenumber".




geocode


geocode(address; kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Forward-geocode address via the Google Geocoding API.

Keyword Arguments

components: component filter string (e.g. "country:US|postal_code:94043").
bounds: viewport bias, "lat1,lng1|lat2,lng2".
language, region: IETF BCP-47 / CLDR codes.
extra::AbstractDict: additional query parameters.
client::GoogleMapsClient: optional; see GoogleMapsClient.
key: API key (default api_key()).
timeout: read timeout in seconds (default 30).






geolocate


geolocate(; kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Locate a device from cell-tower / WiFi data. POST to https://www.googleapis.com/geolocation/v1/geolocate.

Keyword Arguments

home_mobile_country_code, home_mobile_network_code: strings.
radio_type: "lte", "gsm", "cdma", or "wcdma".
carrier: carrier name.
consider_ip: Bool — fall back to IP geolocation.
cell_towers: vector of cell tower dicts.
wifi_access_points: vector of WiFi AP dicts.
client, key, timeout: standard shortcuts.






geometries


geometries(x) -> Vector{Union{GMPoint, GMLineString}}
All non-missing geometries (flattened) from the response.




get_experience_id


get_experience_id(client) — current experience id or nothing.




linestrings


linestrings(x) -> Vector{GMLineString}
All GMLineString geometries from the response.




mapplot


mapplot(x; maptype="roadmap", size=(640, 640), kwargs...) -> Makie.Figure
Plot any GoogleMapsAPI response: fetches a Static Maps background sized to the input’s bounding box, then overlays markers for every lat/lng point found and lines for every encodedPolyline. Accepts any of: geocode, reverse_geocode, compute_routes, compute_route_matrix, elevation, geolocate, snap_to_roads, nearest_roads, places, places_nearby, find_place, place, or any JSON.Object containing one of the recognised shapes.
Method bodies live in the Makie package extension — call sites require using Makie (plus a backend like GLMakie or CairoMakie).
Extra kwargs are forwarded to static_map (e.g. scale=2, language="en", key=..., client=...) except for the plot-styling kwargs markercolor, markersize, linecolor, linewidth, padding.




nearest_roads


nearest_roads(points; kwargs...) -> Vector
Closest road segment for each of up to 100 independent coordinates.




parse_duration


parse_duration(s) -> Float64
Parse a Google duration string (e.g. "16087s", "16087.5s") into seconds. Returns Float64 seconds. Returns NaN for nothing/missing.

Examples
parse_duration("16087s")     # 16087.0
parse_duration("0.5s")       # 0.5





place


place(place_id; fields=nothing, language=nothing, ...) -> JSON.Object
Detailed info for a single place by place_id.




places


places(; query=nothing, location=nothing, radius=nothing, ...) -> JSON.Object
Text-search for places.




places_autocomplete


places_autocomplete(input_text; ...) -> Vector (predictions)




places_autocomplete_query


places_autocomplete_query(input_text; ...) -> Vector (predictions)




places_nearby


places_nearby(; location=nothing, radius=nothing, ...) -> JSON.Object
Nearby-search for places. Either location or page_token is required.




places_photo


places_photo(photo_reference; max_width=nothing, max_height=nothing, ...) -> Vector{UInt8}
Download a place photo’s raw bytes. At least one of max_width/max_height is required.




points


points(x) -> Vector{GMPoint}
All GMPoint geometries from the response, walking into line strings.




reverse_geocode


reverse_geocode(lat, lon; kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
reverse_geocode((lat, lon); kwargs...)
Reverse-geocode a coordinate via the Google Geocoding API.




set_experience_id! {#set_experience_id!}


set_experience_id!(client, id) -> client
set_experience_id!(client, ids::AbstractVector) -> client
Set the X-Goog-Maps-Experience-ID header for subsequent calls. Pass a vector to send a comma-joined list. Pass nothing to clear.




snap_to_roads


snap_to_roads(path; interpolate=false, kwargs...) -> Vector (JSON array)
Snap a GPS path (up to 100 points) to the most likely roads travelled.




snapped_speed_limits


snapped_speed_limits(path; kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Snap path to roads and return the matched road segments with speed limits. Unlike speed_limits, returns the full body (both speedLimits and snappedPoints).




speed_limits


speed_limits(place_ids; kwargs...) -> Vector
Posted speed limits (km/h) for up to 100 road-segment place IDs. place_ids is a string or vector of strings.




static_map


static_map(size; kwargs...) -> Vector{UInt8}
Download a static map image. size is an int (square) or (width, height). Either markers must be provided OR both center and zoom must be set.
markers / path / visible / style accept either a pre-joined string or a vector of pipe-joinable parts. See the Google Static Maps docs for the full list of supported options.




timezone


timezone(location; timestamp=nothing, language=nothing, kwargs...) -> JSON.Object
Get time-zone and UTC-offset info for location at timestamp. location may be any form accepted by the convert.latlng helper. timestamp may be an integer unix time, a DateTime, or a string (default is the current unix time).




waypoint


waypoint(x) -> Dict
Convert x to a Routes API waypoint Dict.
Accepted inputs:

(lat, lon) Tuple or NamedTuple with :lat/:lon (or :latitude/:longitude) → latLng waypoint
AbstractString → address waypoint
AbstractDict → returned as-is (escape hatch for placeId, polyline, etc.)


Examples
